elements.sent-2411-001: Learning STRIPS action models from action traces alone is a challenging problem as it involves learning the domain predicates as well.
elements.sent-2411-002: In this work, a novel approach is introduced which, like the well-known LOCM systems, is scalable, but like SAT approaches, is sound and complete.
elements.sent-2411-003: Furthermore, the approach is general and imposes no restrictions on the hidden domain or the number or arity of the predicates.
elements.sent-2411-004: The new learning method is based on an efficient, novel test that checks whether the assumption that a predicate is affected by a set of action patterns, namely, actions with specific argument positions, is consistent with the traces.
elements.sent-2411-005: The predicates and action patterns that pass the test provide the basis for the learned domain that is then easily completed with preconditions and static predicates.
elements.sent-2411-006: The new method is studied theoretically and experimentally.
elements.sent-2411-007: For the latter, the method is evaluated on traces and graphs obtained from standard classical domains like the 8-puzzle, which involve hundreds of thousands of states and transitions.
elements.sent-2411-008: The learned representations are then verified on larger instances.
elements.sent-2411-009: The problem of learning lifted STRIPS action models from action traces alone is challenging because it requires learning the domain predicates which are not given.
elements.sent-2411-010: The problem has been addressed by the LOCM systems \cite{locm1,locm , and more recently, through SAT and deep learning approaches \cite{bonet:ecai2020,ivan:kr2021,asai:latplan,asai:jair .}}
elements.sent-2411-011: Still all these approaches have severe limitations.
elements.sent-2411-012: The LOCM systems are scalable but heuristic, and their scope is not clear and can fail even in simple domains.
elements.sent-2411-013: The SAT approaches are sound and complete, but they work on graphs, not traces, and more importantly, they do not scale up.
elements.sent-2411-014: Deep learning approaches can deal with traces combining state images and actions, but do not yet produce meaningful lifted representations.
elements.sent-2411-015: In this work, we address the problem using a different approach that combines the benefits of some of these methods while avoiding their pitfalls.
elements.sent-2411-016: Like LOCM, the new method is scalable, and like SAT approaches, it is sound and complete, while learning from either action traces or graphs, without making any assumptions on the ``hidden'' STRIPS domains except that they are ``well formed'', so that action effects must change the state.
elements.sent-2411-017: The new method, named \sift, utilizes an efficient, novel test that checks whether it is consistent with the inputs (traces or graphs) to assume that a predicate can be affected by a set of action patterns; namely, actions with specific argument positions.
elements.sent-2411-018: The approach is evaluated on traces and graphs obtained from standard domains like the 8-puzzle, which involves hundreds of thousands of states and transitions, and the learned domains are verified on larger instances.
elements.sent-2411-019: The rest of the paper is organized as follows: We preview the new ideas through an example, discuss related work, review background notions, and introduce the new learning formulation.
elements.sent-2411-020: Then we present details of the implementation, experimental results, and a summary and discussion.
elements.sent-2411-021: The intuition for the approach is simple.
elements.sent-2411-022: Consider for example the following action trace $\tau$ for the Delivery domain: \begin{equation}pick(o_1,c),move(c,c'),drop(o_1,c'),pick(o_1,c') \ .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-023: This is an applicable sequence of actions in an instance of the domain where an object $o_1$ is picked from a cell $c$, a move is done from $c$ to cell $c'$, the object is dropped, and picked up again.
elements.sent-2411-024: The task is to learn the hidden domain from traces such as this, and this involves learning some predicates, and learning the action schemas for the three domain actions $pick$, $drop$, and $move$, including their effects and preconditions.
elements.sent-2411-025: Given traces like $\tau$ drawn from a hidden domain $D$, the new method will address the learning task by considering questions like the following, where the symbol ``\_'' stands for any values (``don't cares''):.
elements.sent-2411-026: Is the assumption that $D$ involves a unary atom $p(x)$ affected only by actions of the form $pick(x,\_)$ and $drop(x,\_)$, consistent with $\tau$?.
elements.sent-2411-027: Is the assumption that $D$ involves a unary atom $q(x)$ affected only by actions $pick(x,\_)$, consistent with $\tau$?.
elements.sent-2411-028: We will show that these questions can be answered in a crisp and efficient manner, and moreover, that a domain equivalent to the one generating the traces can be obtained from the answers to such questions.
elements.sent-2411-029: The reason is that a ground atom $q(o_1)$ is affected only by actions of the form $pick(o_1,\_)$ is not consistent with the trace $\tau$.
elements.sent-2411-030: Indeed, there are two actions of the form $pick(o_1,\_)$ in the trace, both being instances of the same $pick$ schema, and they must both make the atom $q(o)$ true, or they must both make the atom $q(o_1)$ false.
elements.sent-2411-031: Yet, since the actions $move(c,c')$ and $drop(o_1,c')$ in $\tau$ are assumed not to affect $q(o_1)$, this means that the second $pick(o_1,\_)$ action does not actually change the truth value of $q(o)$ which is in contradiction with the assumption of a well formed domain.
elements.sent-2411-032: Since this situation does not arise for $p(x)$, the answer to the first question is positive.

elements.sent-2411-228: Initially, there is a type $\omega_{a,i}$ for each action $a$ of arity $k_a > 0$ in the traces, and each argument index $i$, $1 \leq i \leq k_a$.
elements.sent-2411-229: Then two types $\omega_{a,i}$ and $\omega_{b,j}$ are merged into one if there is an object $o$ in the traces that appears both as the $i$-th argument of an $a$-action and as the $j$-th argument of a $b$-action.
elements.sent-2411-230: This merging of types is iterated until a fixed point is reached, where the objects mentioned in the traces are partitioned into a set of disjoint types.

elements.sent-2411-252: We have tested the \sift algorithm over a number of benchmarks in classical planning.
elements.sent-2411-253: For this, a set of traces $T$ is generated from one or more instances of a hidden domain $D$, a domain $D_T$ is learned, and $D_T$ is verified over traces $T'$ from larger domain instances.
elements.sent-2411-254: The experiments have been run on two types of Intel(R) Xeon(R) nodes: Platinum 8352M CPU, running at 2.30GHz, and Gold 6330 CPU, running at 2.00GHz, using 22 cores per experiment.
elements.sent-2411-255: The code and the data is to be made publicly available.
elements.sent-2411-256: Domains: include Blocks with 3 and 4 operators, Delivery, Driverlog, Grid, Ferry, Gripper, Hanoi, Logistics, Miconic, Sliding $n$-puzzle, Sokoban.
elements.sent-2411-257: These are all standard domains, some of which have been used in prior work on lifted model learning.
elements.sent-2411-258: Grid-Lock and Sokoban-Pull are variations of Grid and Sokoban, each one adding one action schema to make the resulting domains dead-end free (an extra lock action in Grid, and a pull-action in Sokoban).
elements.sent-2411-259: Dead-ends present a problem for data generation from traces, as most random traces end up being trapped in parts of the state space, failing to reach other parts.
elements.sent-2411-260: Training Data: For each domain, the training data (traces) is obtained from a single large instance $P$ with approximately 100k edges.
elements.sent-2411-261: This size is used to determine the number of objects in the instance $P$ used to generate the data, except for Logistics that required more data.
elements.sent-2411-262: The (plain) traces are sampled using two parameters: the number of traces $n$, and their length $L$ (number of actions).
elements.sent-2411-263: The first trace is sampled from the initial state $s_0$, while the rest are sampled starting in a state $s$ that is reached from $s_0$ in $m$ random steps, $2L \leq m \leq 5L$.
elements.sent-2411-264: The number $n$ of traces has been set to $5$, and the length of the traces have been set roughly to the minimum lengths $L$ needed so that 5 random traces of length $L$ result in learned domains with 100\% validation success rates (as explained below).
elements.sent-2411-265: In some domains, the length $L$ required involves tens of actions, in others, a few hundred.
elements.sent-2411-266: These are all plain traces with no state equalities.
elements.sent-2411-267: A second type of training input is considered for reference which uses full state graphs $G(P)$ as in \cite{bonet:ecai2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-268: This input corresponds to traces augmented with state equalities.
elements.sent-2411-269: The SAT approach can deal with graphs with a few hundred states; as we will see, \sift can deal with hundreds of thousands, without making assumptions that the graph is complete or that different nodes represent different states.
elements.sent-2411-270: Indeed, a third type of training input is considered as well which is given by a subgraph of $G(P)$.
elements.sent-2411-271: For this, a breadth first search is done in $P$ from the initial state and a few other sampled states until a number of states and edges are generated that yield $100\%$ validation success rates.
elements.sent-2411-272: The difference with the plain traces, is that these are extended traces (state equalities) sampled in ``breadth'' and not in ``depth''.

elements.sent-2411-274: In Grid and Sokoban, a single large sample drawn from the initial state of $P$ was used instead, because samples from deeper states fail to reach many other states, as mentioned above.
elements.sent-2411-275: Validation and Verification: For testing validity and generalization, we consider hidden instances $P'=\tup{D,I'}$ that are larger than the instances $P=\tup{D,I}$ used in training, and use the methods above for obtaining a set of traces and of extended traces (partial state graphs) $T'$ from $P'$.
elements.sent-2411-276: For checking if these sets of validation and test traces is compatible with the learned domain $D_T$, we check if there is a node in the input where an action $a(o)$ is done in $T'$ such that a precondition $f(o')$ of $a(o)$ in $D_T$ is found to be false in $n$.
elements.sent-2411-277: If there is no such node, then $T'$ is regarded as being compatible with $D_T$.
elements.sent-2411-278: The reason that we cannot demand the preconditions of $a(o)$ to be true rather than being ``not false'', is that the information in a trace is incomplete.
elements.sent-2411-279: We also test if sequences of actions that are not applicable in $P'$ are not applicable in the learned domain $D_T$ either.

elements.sent-2411-345: A STRIPS problem $P=\tup{D,I}$ defines a state graph $G(P)$ where nodes represent reachable states in $P$, the root node represents the initial state, and edges indicate state transitions labeled with the actions causing them.
elements.sent-2411-346: A path in this graph represents an action sequence that is applicable in the state represented by the first node.

elements.sent-2411-350: An assumption in \sift is that the observed action traces come from a hidden STRIPS domain with negation which is well-formed: \begin{assumption The hidden STRIPS domain $D$ is well-formed in the sense that if an action makes a literal true, the complement of the literal is an action precondition.}
elements.sent-2411-351: The assumption can be enforced by including the complement of effects as preconditions.
elements.sent-2411-352: The result is that action effects always change the state.
elements.sent-2411-353: While a planning domain $D$ is normally described in terms of a set of actions schemas with their preconditions and effects, there is an alternative way of describing action effects without their signs, that will be convenient in our setting.
elements.sent-2411-354: For example, a domain $D$ with two actions schemas \begin{equation}\hbox{Action } a(x_1,x_2): \, \hbox{ Effects } p(x_1,x_2), \hbox{Action } b(x_1,x_2,x_3): \, \hbox{Effects } q(x_1,x_2), q(x_3,x_2)\end{equation} can also be expressed in term of the atoms affected by the actions as: \begin{equation}\hbox{Atom } p(x_1,x_2): \, \hbox{Affected by } a(x_1,x_2) \hbox{Atom } q(x_1,x_2): \, \hbox{Affected by } b(x_1,x_2,\_), b(\_,x_2,x_1) ,\end{equation} where the missing arguments ``$\_$'' are don't cares that can take any value.
elements.sent-2411-355: In the normal representation of action effects, each action schema occurs once and may involve many atoms with the same predicate; in the alternative, dual representation, each lifted atom occurs once and may involve multiple action patterns.
elements.sent-2411-356: A finite set of inputs in the form of action traces determines a finite set of action names $a$ with their arities, and these determine in turn a finite set of action patterns $a$ and a finite set of features.
elements.sent-2411-357: The action grounding $A_f(o)$ of a feature $f$ in a given set $T$ of traces, will refer to the set of ground actions $a(o')$ in $T$ that are assumed to affect the truth of the hypothetical ground atom $f(o)$.
elements.sent-2411-358: Each pattern $a$ in $B_f$ represents an effect on the hypothetical atom $f(x)$, which must hence have a sign: positive ($1$) when the atom becomes true, and negative ($0$) when the atom becomes false.
elements.sent-2411-359: For this, we extract two types of pattern constraints from sets of traces $T$: those that follow from patterns appearing sequentially in some grounding of $T$, and those that follow from patterns appearing in parallel in some grounding of $T$.
elements.sent-2411-360: If $a$ and $b[t']$ affect $f$ and are consecutive in $T$, the two patterns must have different signs.
elements.sent-2411-361: A different type of constraint on action patterns arises from (extended) traces that start at or reach the same state:.
elements.sent-2411-362: If the action patterns $a$ and $b[t']$ in $B_f$ express a fork in $T$ arising from a state $s$ or converging to a state $s'$, they will have the same $f(o)$ precondition in $s$ or the same $f(o)$ effect in $s'$, and in either case, {they must have the same sign.}
elements.sent-2411-363: The set of constraints resulting from a set of plain or extended traces is the following:.
elements.sent-2411-364: The set of $C_f(T)$ of pattern constraints is given by the inequality constraints $sign(a) \not= sign(b[t'])$ for consecutive patterns $a$ and $b[t']$ from $B_f$ in $T$, and by the equality constraints $sign(a) = sign(b[t'])$ for fork patterns $a$ and $b[t']$ from $B_f$ in $T$.
elements.sent-2411-365: Two ``border conditions'' need to be handled as well.
elements.sent-2411-366: We will also regard two patterns $a$ and $b[t']$ in $B_f$ as a fork if there is an action in some grounding $A_f(o)$ which is an instance of the two patterns (for this, $a=b$), and as consecutive patterns if instances of $a$ and $b[t']$ are the first and last action in a grounding $A_f(o)$ of a (sub) trace that connects two states which are connected by another (sub) trace(s) with an empty grounding $A_f(o)$.
elements.sent-2411-367: In the first case, the patterns $a$ and $b[t']$ in $B_f$ must have the same sign because they account for the effect of the same ground action over $f(o)$, while in the second case, the patterns must have different signs because the same $f(o)$ literal must be a precondition and negative effect of the first action, and a positive effect of the second.
elements.sent-2411-368: For this, notice that a trace in $T$ with a non-empty action grounding $A_f(o)$, defines a unique truth value, up to negation, for the atom $f(o)$ in every state of the trace.
elements.sent-2411-369: Likewise, in a set of connected (extended) traces where each pair of traces shares a common state, a non-empty grounding $A_f(o)$ in one of the traces, determines a unique truth value for the atom $f(o)$, up to negation, in every state of all of the connected traces.\footnote{ A trace with a non-empty action grounding $A_f(o)$ defines the truth value of $f(o)$ right after and right before any action $a(o') \in A_f(o)$; the first is the sign of the pattern $a$ in $B_f$ for which $o=t[o']$, the latter is the inverse sign.}
elements.sent-2411-370: A set of traces $T$ is complete for an instance $P=\tup{D,I}$ if the traces in $T$ are all drawn from the initial state of $P$ and hence are connected, they affect each predicate $p$ in $D$, and $I$ contains the same atoms as $A(D,T)$, ignoring the signs.

elements.sent-2411-377: We have tested \sift over a number of benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-378: The experiments have been run on two types of Intel(R) Xeon(R) nodes: Platinum 8352M CPU, running at 2.30GHz, and Gold 6330 CPU, running at 2.00GHz, using 22 cores.
elements.sent-2411-379: The code and data are public \cite{goesgensjansen:siftcodeupload .}
elements.sent-2411-380: Dead-ends present a problem for data generation, as most random traces end up being trapped in parts of the state space.
elements.sent-2411-381: Training Data: For each domain, the training data (traces) is obtained from a single large instance $P$ with approximately 100k edges.\footnote{Traces from multiple instances could have been used too.}
elements.sent-2411-382: The number $n$ of traces has been set to $5$, and the length of the traces have been set roughly to the minimum lengths $L$ needed so that 5 random traces of length $L$ result in learned domains with $100\%$ validation success rates (as explained below).
elements.sent-2411-383: Plain traces fail in Grid and Sokoban (see below).
elements.sent-2411-384: The scalability of \sift shows when processing full state graphs with 500,000 edges or more, as in the $n$-puzzle and Logistics.
elements.sent-2411-385: In almost all domains, the number of admissible features learned $\#F_a$ is slightly larger than the number of (non-static) predicates $\#P$, meaning that some ``redundant'' predicates from $D_{max}$ are learned along with the hidden predicates in $D$.
elements.sent-2411-386: Grid and Sokoban are not learned correctly from random traces, even if Grid-Lock and Sokoban-Pull, which contain all of their action schemas, are.
elements.sent-2411-387: The reason is that the extra actions remove the dead-ends which prevent random traces from being sufficiently informative.
elements.sent-2411-388: The difficulty in Grid and Sokoban is thus not learning but sampling.
elements.sent-2411-389: Interestingly, Grid also illustrates a case in which plain traces are strictly less informative than extended traces.
elements.sent-2411-390: Indeed, one can show that Grid cannot be learned correctly from plain traces alone.
elements.sent-2411-391: The reason is that in any trace, a locked cell is opened once from a specific cell, and there is no way to determine that the same state can be reached by opening the lock from another cell.
elements.sent-2411-392: Extended traces can represent state equality and avoid this problem, while plain traces do not cause this problem in Grid-Lock, where a cell can be locked again and reopened from a different adjacent cell.. Analysis.
elements.sent-2411-393: In the $n$-sliding puzzle, \sift learns 24 ``redundant'' but meaningful features (details in the extended version).
elements.sent-2411-394: We have presented the first general, and scalable solution to the problem of learning lifted STRIPS models from action traces alone.
elements.sent-2411-395: The consistent features define the learned domain which is guaranteed to generalize correctly for a suitable finite set of traces.
elements.sent-2411-396: The experiments show the generality and scalability of the approach.
elements.sent-2411-397: Three direct extensions that we have not addressed in the paper are: the the elimination of ``redundant'' features and predicates, the derivation of static predicates of lower arity, and the variations needed to make the learning approach robust to noisy inputs.
